title: Western Civilization to 1660
description: (HIST 2311) Western Civilization to 1660. CONTENT. Ancient civilizations, Greek, Roman and Asian; Christianity; medieval civilization in west, eastern Europe; political, social and intellectual developments from earliest human cultures to 1660. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce

title: Western Civilization Since 1660
description: (HIST 2312) Western Civilization Since 1660. CONTENT. Religious, dynastic and imperial developments; Industrial Revolution; western democracies; rise of nationalism and communism; central and eastern Europe; intellectual revolution; World Wars I and II and the contemporary world. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. title: World History to 1500
description: (HIST 2321) World History to 1500. CONTENT. Development of major world societies in the premodern era; emergence of agrarian-based modes of production, political states, religious economy and a global division of systems; Eurasian world system and the civilizations of Africa and the Americas. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce

title: World History Since 1500
description: (HIST 2322) World History Since 1500. CONTENT. Formations of major world societies and their interactions in the modern era; emergence of the modern world economy and a global division of labor. Themes include, but are not limited to, changes in devotional practices (Reformations); premodern empires; trade and global exchanges; political revolutions and their global impacts; industrialization and resultant ideologies; imperialism, colonialism and reactions in Africa, Asia and Latin America; world wars; genocides; socialist experimentation; and the Cold War. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce

title: History of the United States to 1877
description: (HIST 1301) History of the United States to 1877. CONTENT. History of United States from colonial origins through Reconstruction, examining major political, economic and social developments. Themes include but are not limited to the Revolution, Constitution, nationalism, sectionalism and Civil War. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Galveston and Qatar campuses

title: History of the United States Since 1877
description: (HIST 1302) History of the United States Since 1877. CONTENT. History of United States since Reconstruction, examining major political, economic and social developments. Themes include but are not limited to industrialization, progressivism, emergence as a world power, World Wars I and II, the New Deal and contemporary America. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Galveston and Qatar campuses

title: Introduction to Public History
description: Examination of the methods, practices, and approaches of Public History; exploration of how historians work with and influence the development of museums, libraries, and archives; historic markers, historic sites, and monuments; exhibits, artwork, and oral history; television, film, and podcasts/radio, digital maps, virtual reality, and programming

title: The Human Experience
description: Introduction to classic transformative texts in the history of the arts, sciences, and humanities; interdisciplinary methods and approaches within the humanities; key ethical and moral debates across the human experience. Cross Listing: ARSC 202 and PHIL 202

title: Famous Trials in American History
description: History of prominent legal trials in the United States; role of the media in legal discourse; overview of changing ideas about crime, punishment, and justice; examination of trials as a mirror onto social, political, and cultural trends

title: Introduction to Russian History
description: CONTENT. Russian history, culture and society from origins to the present; rise of the Russian Empire; autocracy; modernization without liberalization; reforms, reaction, revolution; development of Communist regime; continuity from Imperial to Soviet period in industrialization, bureaucracy and treatment of peasants, nationalities and intellectual opposition; Gorbachev and a new "revolution." Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. title: History of Britain to the 18th Century
description: CONTENT. Political, social, economic and cultural history of the British Isles from pre-history to the 18th century. Themes include but are not limited to British, Saxon and Norman origins; national development; struggles between church and state; crown and nobles; nobles and commons; development of Parliament; social and economic developments; Reformation and Civil War; interactions with the wider world. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. title: History of Britain From the 18th Century
description: CONTENT. Political, social, economic and cultural history of the British Isles since the 18th century. Themes include but are not limited to agrarian and Industrial Revolutions; relations with Ireland; evolution of democracy; struggles with France and Napoleon; social legislation in the 20th century; growth of Empire until World War II; interactions with the wider world and domestic developments since World War II. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. title: History of Christianity I - Origins to the Reformation
description: CONTENT. History of Christianity from its inception until the beginnings of European colonial expansion in the first half of the 16th century. Themes include the teachings of Jesus and his disciples; the growth of Christianity as it spread throughout the Mediterranean basin into Mesopotamia, Africa, Northern Europe and central Asia; variations in doctrinal development and religious practice in diverse social, political and geographical situations. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Cross Listing: CLAS 220 and RELS 220

title: Revolutionary America
description: CONTENT. History of the American Revolution. Themes include but are not limited to cultural conflict; loyalists; civil war dimensions; ideological debates; British perspectives; experiences of African slaves, American Indians and women; the revolution’s impact on political, social, cultural and economic developments in the United States through the end of the 18th century. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. title: History of Texas
description: (HIST 2301) History of Texas. CONTENT. History of Texas from pre-Columbian cultures to Spanish and other European settlements, Mexican era and Texas independence, through annexation and development of Texas as a modern-day state. Themes include, but are not limited to, military conflicts and contributions; land tenure, labor, family dynamics; Texas’ multiple borders and migration; socio-cultural interactions; changing legal structure; education; farming and ranching; oil discoveries and industrialization; Texas’ music, sports and popular culture. Emphasis placed on challenges and triumphs of Texans of all types throughout the years. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Galveston campus

title: American Military History
description: CONTENT. The evolution of American military history from the colonial period to the present, examining the causes, conduct and consequences of war and the armed forces’ relationship to society, politics and culture. Themes include but are not limited to the Revolutionary War, Civil War, world wars, Cold War conflicts and 21st-century operations. Emphasis on political and military leadership, soldiers' experiences with war and the changing character of warfare in shaping the United States’ military institutions and national identity. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Qatar campus

title: The Second World War: Origins, Course, and Consequences
description: Rise of fascism in Europe and East Asia; causes and consequences of World War Two; strategies, tactics, and battles in the European and Pacific theaters; civilian lives and the homefront for Allied and Axis powers

title: American Sea Power
description: CONTENT. The history of American sea power from colonial beginnings to the present, examining the U.S. Navy’s evolution as a profession of arms, maritime strategy, technology and the relationship between sea power and national policy. Themes include but are not limited to privateering, naval warfare, commerce, exploration and global presence from the Age of Sail through modern carrier and submarine forces. Emphasis on how maritime strength shaped America’s security, economy and role in world affairs. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Galveston campus

title: European Military History
description: CONTENT. European military history from the Thirty Years’ War to the end of the Cold War, examining the development of warfare, strategy, technology and state power in shaping modern Europe. Themes include but are not limited to the rise of professional armies, the Napoleonic Wars, the World Wars and the evolution of total war and nuclear deterrence in the Cold War era. Emphasis on the interaction between military institutions, political change and the social impact of conflict. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. title: Empires of Food
description: CONTENT. Examination of the relationship between food, imperialism and colonization in historical context. Topics include but are not limited to foodways, culture and society; environmental impact of colonial agriculture; sugar and slavery; food production and labor; food security; protest; food chains; and consumerism. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. title: Sport in National and International History
description: Sport in politics, culture, and region; race, place, gender, and identity in athletic history; sports as an expression of nationalism, globalism, commerce, and media

title: United States Maritime History
description: Development of American maritime history from colonial times to the present; Galveston campus

title: American Indian History
description: CONTENT. Native North American history from earliest habitation to the present; addresses widely varying Indigenous groups; themes include but are not limited to cultural exchange, colonial conflict, U.S. Indian policy, resistance and revitalization, sovereignty and self-determination, activism. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. Also taught at Qatar campus

title: The Historian’s Craft
description: CONTENT. Sophomore seminar. Instructors select different topics and assign distinct readings to meet the learning objectives. All sections address issues related to the work of the professional historian, including archival research, types of historical evidence, analysis and argumentation and the principles and methods of analytical narrative. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: History majors.

title: Directed Studies
description: Selected fields of history not covered in depth by other courses. Reports and extensive reading required
prerequisites: Approval of department head.

title: Special Topics in...
description: Selected topics in an identified area of history. May be repeated for credit
prerequisites: Approval of instructor.

title: Research
description: Research conducted under the direction of faculty member in history
prerequisites: 24 hours if history, with 12 or more at 200-level or above; freshman or sophomore classification and approval of instructor.

title: History and Memory
description: Relationships between past and present; role of change in political, social and cultural contexts across time; social and public functions of historical research; contested relationship between professional historians, politicians and states, cultural institutions, the media and the general public
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval from instructor.

title: Southwest Borderlands
description: CONTENT. Origins and development of Indigenous, Spanish and Mexican history of Greater Southwest; exploration and conquest; Spanish entradas into Southwest; rise of institutions and colonial society; economic history; examination of social and cultural relations including gender; Mexican independence; Mexico's far northern frontier, 1821-1848. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Chicana/o History since 1848
description: Social, economic and political evolution of Chicanas/os from 1848 to present; includes current issues, legacies of violence, land tenure systems, racial discrimination, changing class relations, gender, civil rights, immigration, identity, and culture
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Latinx History
description: Latinx communities from the nineteenth century to the present in the U.S.; Mexican Americans, Puerto Ricans, Cubans, and Central and South Americans; differences in historical experiences; role of race, class, politics, immigration, gender and sexuality; cultural identity as expressed in art, literature, folklore and religion; contemporary social, political and economic issues
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: American Indians in the U.S. South
description: CONTENT. Examination of the role of indigenous populations in the history and formation of the U.S. South; cultural values and social practices; impact of European settlement and African slavery; trade patterns, imperial wars and removal policies; citizenship, recognition and enrollment politics. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Latino/a Labor in the United States
description: The experience of Latino/a labor in the United States, from the 19th through the early 21st century; role of gender, race and ethnicity and policy on labor experiences and labor relations; intersections of labor and migration, globalization
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Born Country - American Agricultural History
description: History of American agricultural settlement and development from the colonial period to the present; labor, technology, the environment, politics and government policy; regional variations; farmer rebellions; rural society and culture
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: U.S. Immigration and Ethnicity
description: The sources and persistence of ethnic identity in 19th and 20th century America; its interaction with religion, politics, languages, education and social mobility; various nativist and anti-immigrant movements; contrasts and continuities between contemporary immigration patterns and those of earlier eras

title: History of the Atlantic World
description: Introduction to the comparative study of the civilizations and cultures that bordered on the Atlantic Ocean; examination of culture and economic exchanges and adaptations, migrations, empire-building, and the emergence of new societies and cultures
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: The Age of Revolution in the Atlantic World
description: CONTENT. Examination of the origins and course of the revolutions that transformed the Atlantic Empires of Great Britain, France and Spain in the late 18th and 19th centuries, including the American, French, Haitian and Spanish revolutions as well as the Latin American independence movements; disruption of old political and economic orders; creation of independent states in the Americas; dynamics of sovereignty, rights and citizenship; social and cultural developments. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: History of the Iberian World
description: Introduction to the people and places of the Iberian World, ca. 1500-1900; social, political and economical relations between Spain, Portugal, Asia and the Americas; emergence of a shared culture and cross-cultural exchange
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Asian American History
description: Asian migration to the Americas; East Asian, Southeast Asian, South Asian and Pacific Islander American perspectives; role of race, class, gender, and sexuality; civil movements for immigration and citizenship rights; cultural expression and identity formation
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: History of the Caribbean
description: History of the Caribbean region from human settlement to the present; Indigenous peoples; European colonization; colonial societies; challenges to the imperial plantation model; emancipation; independence and decolonization
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Women in Ancient Greece and Rome
description: Survey of women in classical Greece and Rome; emphases on female occupations and family relationships, legal and political status, traditional values, notorious women, how women were viewed and how they viewed themselves
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: CLAS 330 and WGST 330 .

title: Medieval Mediterranean, 300-1453
description: History of the European, North African and Middle Eastern states of the Mediterranean region in the medieval period; emphasis on political, religious, cultural, social, economic, environmental, scientific, institutional and diplomatic issues of the area and its hinterlands
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: History of Europe in the Nineteenth Century
description: Cultural, economic, and political processes that shaped European civilization (east and west); the Napoleon era; industrialization and urbanization; liberalism and socialism; empire and revolution; cultural developments
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Europe Since 1919
description: CONTENT. Examines Europe’s transformations from the aftermath of World War I to the present. Themes include but are not limited to the rise and fall of fascist and communist regimes; interwar crises; World War II and the Holocaust; postwar reconstruction; decolonization; the Cold War; European integration; migration and identity; memory politics; and the legacies of 1989. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: War and Society in Europe Since 1900
description: War and social change in Europe during the twentieth century; relationships between front lines and home fronts; government and civil society; gender and war; ethnic and national identities in Eastern, Central, and Western Europe
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Eastern Europe
description: Eastern Europe from the late middle ages to the present; Ottoman, Habsburg, Russian and Soviet Empires; origins of modern East European nation-states; world wars; rise of nationalism; experience of communism and the Cold War; collapse of communism; post-communist democratic and economic transitions
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Global Communism - Its Rise, Fall and Legacies
description: History of communism and the movements and states it inspired across the globe; communism’s intellectual origins and development throughout the world; everyday life; state violence and communism at war; race, gender, nationalism and environmental impacts; the decline, collapse and reinvention of communism; contested legacies and relevance of communism today
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Latin America to 1810
description: Spanish and Portuguese colonization in the Americas; encounters between Indigenous, African and European peoples; everyday lives of individual women and men in the Iberian colonies; cultural, social, economic and political developments within colonial Latin America
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Latin America Since 1810
description: Political history of independent South American nations since independence with emphasis upon ABC countries; economic, social and cultural development; foreign relations
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Inter-American Relations
description: Cultural, diplomatic and economic relations in the Western Hemisphere in historical perspective
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Modern Middle East
description: Survey of the Middle East since 1800; introduction to Islam and Islamic civilization; decline of the Ottoman Empire; European imperialism; rise of nationalist movements; Zionism and the emergence of Israel; Arab-Israeli conflict; impact of oil; revolution in Iran and Islamic resurgence
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: American Environmental History
description: History of American attitudes toward nature: use of land, water, timber, oil, coal, wildlife and other natural resources in the United States; conservation movement and significant conflicts over resources; changing perception of the physical environment

title: History of Energy in America
description: Impact of energy upon industrial America; emphasis on relationship between energy and industrial development, emergence of state and federal energy policies, role of energy in foreign policy, growth of energy-oriented industries and impact of energy development on the environment

title: History of Science
description: The ideas of the great scientists and their impact on society; the Newtonian Revolution; Lavoisier and the new chemistry; Darwin and evolutionary thought; Enrico Fermi, Robert Oppenheimer and the development of nuclear energy

title: History of Science in America
description: The major developments in the physical and life sciences from colonial times to the present; the lives and scientific contributions of such famous American scientists as Benjamin Franklin, Joseph Henry, Thomas Edison and J. Robert Oppenheimer

title: American Colonies
description: Colonization of North America and the Caribbean; interactions between diverse Indigenous, African and European peoples; development of colonial societies and the creation of an Atlantic world
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: The Birth of the Republic, 1763-1820
description: Impact of French and Indian War; British colonial policy 1763-1775; War for Independence; Confederation crisis; Constitution-making and ratification; development of political parties; problem of foreign entanglements; War of 1812; conflict of nationalist and sectionalist tendencies; historiography and interpretation

title: The United States, 1820-1860
description: Jacksonian democracy; impact of nationalism and sectionalism; manifest destiny and Mexican War; slavery controversy; expansion

title: Civil War and Reconstruction
description: Survey of background and causes of the war; military, political, economic, and diplomatic aspects of the war; life behind the lines; Reconstruction and post-war adjustments, 1861-1877

title: America in the Gilded Age, 1877-1901
description: CONTENT. Examination of the Gilded Age, the period between the end of the Civil War in 1865 and the dawn of the new century in 1900. Topics include, but are not limited to, the technological revolution; the age of industry; reactions to modernism; labor movements; politics in the 1870s and 1880s; the rise of Jim Crow; urbanization; immigration and nativism; new women; moral reformers; the Spanish-American War and its aftermath. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Reform, War and Normalcy: The United States, 1901-1929
description: Emergence of Progressivism; reform in the cities and states; reforms and foreign policies of the Theodore Roosevelt, William Howard Taft and Woodrow Wilson administrations; World War I and aftermath; Harding-Coolidge normalcy; the Jazz Age; Hoover and the Great Crash

title: The Great Depression and World War II
description: The United States, 1929-1945; cultural, social, economic, and political developments in the nation; global diplomacy and military strategy; also taught at Galveston campus

title: The United States After World War II
description: The United States since World War II; political, economic, cultural and social changes and role as a world leader; also taught at Galveston campus

title: Great Scientists in History
description: History of fundamental scientific principles through biography; Galileo, Newton, Darwin, Mendel, Curie, Einstein, Pauling, and others
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Africana Women's History
description: Black women’s history from the precolonial era to the present; emphasis on the cultural, political, legal, economic, sexual, social, and religious factors that shaped their experiences across the African Diaspora and the world
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: AFST 377 and WGST 377 .

title: Afro-Latin America
description: History and cultures of the Black Diaspora in Latin America from colonization through the twentieth century; social, political, cultural, and economic impact of Africans and their descendants on the region; emphasis on race, culture, citizenship, and identity
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Modern Korea
description: History of modern Korea; South Korea; North Korea; Korea through imperialism, decolonization, a civil war, dictatorship, and democratization; emphasis on cultural, social, economic, and political developments
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: The Mongols in World History
description: Examination of the origins, development, and legacies of the largest contiguous empire in history; exploration of the environmental context, notable rulers, Mongol warfare, imperial governance and administration, interactions between the Mongols and rival nomadic groups, trade, gender and the role of women, religious and ideological policies, and long-term impacts
prerequisites: Junior or senior classiﬁcation; or approval of instructor.

title: Energy and the Environment - Conflict or Compatibility
description: Examination of history of renewable and non-renewable energy sources and their impact on the environment; focuses on the research of scientists and the scientific-technological, political, economic, and cultural issues that emerged in the 1800s-2000s; investigation of whether production and consumption of non-renewable fossil fuel energy sources are compatible or in conflict with the scientific community’s environmental concerns
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Sickness and Health in U.S. History
description: CONTENT. Examination of medicine and health in the U.S., from colonial times to present; social history of medicine; experiences of patients and providers; medical ethics; U.S. health systems. This upper-level elective course focuses on the history of health and sickness in the United States, guided by questions about the meaning of health and sickness, changing ideas of illness over time and changing medical institutions and patient experiences. Themes explored may include public health, the development of medical professions and institutions, medical expertise and authority, disease and diagnosis, health and politics and the relationship between health care and broader structures of inequality, identity and access in American society. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: World War I
description: CONTENT. History of World War I; diplomacy and the causes of the conflict; conduct of the war; experience of the war on the home fronts; historical controversies in interpreting the major issues of the war; how the war affected the world, exploring foundational impacts on major diplomatic, social, cultural, political and economic changes of the 20th century that continue to reverberate today. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classiﬁcation or approval of instructor.

title: Slavery in World History
description: Comparative history of human slavery; slavery in the Ancient World, Asia, Africa; varieties of modern slavery in the New World since 1500; abolition of slavery and continuing forms of human bondage in the contemporary world
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: AFST 401 and ASIA 401 .

title: History of Modern Germany
description: Germany from the late eighteenth century to the present; cultural, social and political developments in national and transnational context

title: Post 1945 Germanies
description: Examines Germany from the end of World War II to the end of the 20th century; includes political, social, cultural, and economic life in divided and occupied Germany; covers Germany since reunification in 1990
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: History of the Holocaust
description: History of the Nazi Holocaust; Third Reich; Jewish Ghetto life and concentration camps; role of the military, S.S. and German business; lessons and legacies; also taught at Galveston campus

title: Revolutionary and Napoleonic France
description: CONTENT. Social, cultural, economic and political dynamics in continental France and the French Caribbean between 1750 and 1815. Themes include, but are not limited to, changing relationships between church and state; development of human and social rights; race and slavery; expansion of civil institutions during the Napoleonic Consulate and Empire; and changes in military practices. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: France Since 1815
description: CONTENT. Social, cultural, economic and political dynamics in continental France and the French Empire since 1815. Themes include, but are not limited to, changing relationships between church and state; industrialization and its impact on social status and political ideologies; political revolutions; imperialism in Africa and Indochina; global world wars; and decolonization. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Russian History to 1801
description: Origins and Christianization of Russia; establishment and decline of Kievan Rus' state; Mongol conquest and domination of Russia; rise of Moscow, establishment of tsardom, expansion of state in sixteenth and seventeenth centuries; Peter the Great's reforms; emergence of Russian Empire as a major power; era of Catherine the Great

title: Imperial Russia 1801-1917
description: The last century of the autocratic Romanov dynasty and the social, intellectual, economic and political forces that ended it; political culture, society in transition, international affairs and revolutionary groups in an era of reform, counter-reform, reaction and industrialization

title: Russia's Long 20th Century - The Soviet Experiment and Beyond
description: CONTENT. The evolution of the Soviet experiment, beginning with the late Russian Empire and the war and revolution that ended it; the Bolshevik Party's seizure of power; the resulting socialist revolution; Stalin and Stalinism; Soviet society and everyday life; culture and the arts; Soviet involvement in World War II and the Cold War; opposition, dissent, reform and collapse of the USSR; post-Soviet developments and rise of Putin; the legacy and continued relevance of the Soviet experiment today. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Intellectual History from the Ancient Near East to the Early Middle Ages
description: Political, social, cultural and religious histories of significant figures, groups, schools of thought and movements in western Afro-Eurasia from the Assyrian Empire to the later Roman Empire; developments in political theory, literature, sociology, arts, architecture, music, philosophy, law, sciences and education
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: CLAS 418 and RELS 418 .

title: The Nation of Ireland, 1800 to the Present
description: History of Ireland from the creation of the United Kingdom through the end of the twentieth century; British-Irish relations; agrarian unrest and violence; famine; political, cultural, and religious developments
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Digital Humanities Theory and Practice
description: The use of digital tools for conducting humanities research; theoretical ideas that inform the field; application of theory to the critical assessment of online digital projects in the field
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing: DHUM 433 and ENGL 433 .

title: Seventeenth-Century Britain
description: Social, political, economic, cultural and religious developments, Puritanism and the Revolution of the 1640s, the Restoration, establishment of constitutional monarchy after 1688, Great Britain's rise as an imperial power

title: Eighteenth Century Britain
description: Political, social, economical, intellectual, cultural, and imperial history of Britain in the eighteenth century
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Nineteenth Century Britain
description: Political, social, economic, cultural, intellectual and military history of Great Britain from 1815 to 1914
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Twentieth Century Britain
description: Constitutional, political, economic, military, social and cultural history of Great Britain since 1900
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: History of Mexico, 1821 to the Present
description: Political, economic and social development of Mexico since independence and her relation to other world powers

title: World War II
description: Origins; military campaigns in Europe, North Africa, Asia, and the Pacific; European, Japanese, Asian, and American home fronts; collapse of Germany; atomic warfare; legacies
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: American Military History to 1901
description: American military experience from colonial days to 1901; causes, nature and effect of the wars in which the United States has participated

title: American Military History Since 1901
description: American military experience from 1901 to present; causes, nature and effect of wars in which the United States has participated; effect of war on American history

title: History of Military Strategy
description: Military thought and theory, 1700 to the present
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Law and Society in the United States through Reconstruction
description: How political and social conditions in American history have produced fundamental constitutional principles, changes and practices; historical evolution of written and unwritten Constitution; state law, legal theory, legal profession and private law through the Civil War and Reconstruction
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: American Law Since 1865
description: Analysis of themes in American legal and constitutional history since 1865; examination of social, cultural, and political context surrounding major Supreme Court decisions; social impact of laws and Supreme Court decisions; investigation of legal activities such as moot court and amicus curiae briefs
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: History of Brazil, 1822 to the Present
description: Political, cultural and economic development of Brazil since independence; slavery and race relations; relation to other world powers
prerequisites: Junior classification.

title: Southern Identities and Cultures Since Reconstruction
description: Focus on the aftermath of slavery and defeat in those parts of North America where slavery dominated the economy, politics and demographics; transformations in race, culture and politics in such regions and emergence of new identities since Reconstruction; debates over the geographic and cultural roots of the American South
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: The American Revolution
description: The causes and consequences of revolutionary activity; battlefields and homefronts during the War for Independence; social, political and economic impact of the Revolution on diverse peoples and communities both in the new United States and abroad; historical memory of the American Revolution
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: The American West
description: Patterns of westward expansion and settlement; Native American history and culture; global immigration to the region; Western political, economic, social, and environmental developments; the American West in popular culture
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: The History of Childhood and Family in America
description: History of childhood and family in American history; examination of how region, race, ethnicity, class and gender shape children's and the family's experiences; consideration of how social, cultural, economic, and political structures shape ideas about and activities of children and families in America

title: American Society and Culture Since 1877
description: CONTENT. Examination of the evolution of social and political thought and its influence on American institutions and culture since 1877. Themes include, but are not limited to, access to and content of higher education; sports as sites of participation and fandom; music as an industry and cultural product; religion and its relationship to American politics; advertising as both an economic engine and a reflection of cultural priorities. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. Instructors may assign different readings and/or topics that fit within the course description and meet the learning objectives. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: American Foreign Relations to 1913
description: History of U.S. foreign relations and policies to 1913
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: American Foreign Relations Since 1913
description: History of U.S. foreign relations and policies since 1913
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: International Developments Since 1918
description: General survey of world politics since close of World War I; problems and ideologies of great powers of Europe and factors and conditions which explain present political tendencies and policies

title: American Business History
description: Management strategy and structure of the modern corporation in 19th and 20th century America and the corporation's changing roles in American society

title: Survey of World History for Elementary Education Certification
description: World history for future social studies instructors; politics, economics, religion, and culture in global context; instructional methods and pedagogical philosophy for prospective elementary-level educators
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Programming for Historians
description: Exploration of the use of computers for historical research; creating data out of historical source material; computer programming skills for digitizing, querying, and sharing historical texts
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Topics in Historical Ethnic Conflict
description: Analysis of a specific instance of historical ethnic conflict; examination of the social, political, religious, and economic factors contributing to that historical conflict; discussion of structural factors influencing ethnic, national and other group efforts to advance claims to separate identity and self-government; case study will vary by semester
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.

title: Empires in History
description: Empires in their formative, medieval, early modern and modern periods within a comparative framework with a case-study approach; geographic range varies; politics, religion, culture, literature, arts, economics, environment, race, gender, identity formation, technology, class, medicine, sciences, philosophy, labor, violence, agriculture, immigration, slavery, diplomacy and industrialization. May be taken 3 times for credit
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification.
repeatability: May be taken 3 times for credit

title: Immigration in Post-1945 European History
description: History of immigration in post-1945 Europe; experiences of migrants to Europe; migrant participation in workforce; backlash to immigration; migrants’ cultural contributions to Europe; transformation of Europe and European identity
prerequisites: Junior or senior classiﬁcation.

title: Seminar
description: CONTENT. Capstone seminar. Instructors select different topics and assign distinct readings to meet the learning objectives. All sections include the use of primary source materials related to the topic of the seminar; problems of bibliography, historiography and historical method; scholarship concerning a particular issue, event, period or people in history; and instruction and experience in historical writing. Course content is selected to align with the course description and the current state of scholarship in the field. The presentation of course content fosters critical thinking in the context of scholarly consensus and debate, rather than instructors’ views, values or beliefs. BENEFITS. History teaches evaluation and synthesis of evidence, development of persuasive arguments in written and oral form, expansion of understandings of historical contexts that have contributed to human complexity past and present, including but not limited to the formation of group and individual identities, social and political dynamics, and debates about values and rights. Applying this knowledge produces more informed citizens, who are proficient in the abilities required by today’s workforce. May be repeated for credit
prerequisites: Grade of C or better in HIST 280 ; 21 credits of history, 9 of which must be 300-level or above; senior classification or approval of instructor.

title: Internship
description: Internship in a public or private organization to provide students with opportunities to develop career readiness through communication, professional responsibility and research experience appropriate to career objectives. Students are encouraged to take responsibility for their own learning by seeking out possible internship opportunities, designed to enhance understanding of established professional standards and protocols, that best suit their career interests and schedules and to consult with the internship coordinator sooner rather than later, before ﬁnalizing any internship arrangement. May be taken three times for credit. Must be taken on a satisfactory/unsatisfactory basis
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification and approval of internship coordinator.
repeatability: May be taken three times for credit.

title: Directed Studies
description: Selected fields of history not covered in depth by other courses. Reports and extensive reading required
prerequisites: Approval of instructor and academic advisor; also taught at Galveston campus.

title: Special Topics in...
description: Selected topics in an identified area of history. May be repeated for credit

title: Research
description: Research conducted under the direction of faculty member in history
prerequisites: 24 hours of history, with 12 or more at 300-level or above; junior or senior classification; approval of instructor and academic advisor.

title: Independent Honors Studies
description: Directed independent studies for upper division Honors students, regardless of academic major, in selected aspects of history
prerequisites: Junior or senior classification either as Honors student or with overall GPR of 3.25 and letter of approval from head of student's major department, approval of History department head, and approval of both instructor of record and History academic advisor.
